items.host_position: Verified. The Build option's assumptions[0] does assume a single-quarter hard cutover with no dual-run, and its risks[] array has no migration-slippage entry. Re-ran the cost comparison with a one-quarter dual-run added: Build's advantage over Buy narrows from 18% to 4% but still wins, so the recommendation holds once the risk is made explicit.
items.reviewer_position: The single-quarter, no-dual-run assumption is load-bearing and unhedged; the cost comparison could invert under a real dual-run.

verification.detail: Confirmed assumptions[0] and the absent migration risk in options[1] of the decision record.
Re-scored the record's own cost spreadsheet under a dual-run scenario; Build margin dropped 18% -> 4%.

items.resolution: Added a migration-slippage risk with a dual-run contingency to options[1], and recorded the dual-run cost scenario. Recommendation (Build) unchanged, now with the risk visible.

items.host_position: The mismatch is real — 'Strategic Context' names speed-to-market the top factor while the weights table sets cost 0.40 > time-to-market 0.20 — but whether that is an error or a deliberate choice is not something the record settles. It depends on what the business actually intends to prioritize this year.
items.reviewer_position: The scoring silently trades away the priority the memo declares most important; the weights should be reconciled with the stated urgency.

verification.detail: The weights table (cost 0.40, time-to-market 0.20) does contradict the stated 'speed to launch is the single most important factor this year.'

escalation.host_position: The inconsistency is verified, but the fix is a priority call, not a factual correction — raise the time-to-market weight, or justify cost's higher weight.
escalation.reviewer_position: The declared strategic priority (speed) should govern the weights unless there is a stated reason it shouldn't.
escalation.operator_question: The memo calls speed-to-market the top priority this year, but its scoring weights cost higher. Which should actually govern the decision — protect runway (weight cost), or maximize speed-to-market (weight time-to-market)? Your answer changes which option wins.
